title: Principles of Abundance
summary: A foundational overview of the core ideas that shape a post-scarcity worldview — cooperation, regenerative systems, and the commons.

content.body: A living index of the foundational ideas used throughout the wiki.

The principles of abundance describe a way of organising people, tools, and ecosystems so that the default outcome is enough — enough food, enough energy, enough care, enough time. They are drawn from regenerative agriculture, commons theory, cooperative economics, and the lived practice of communities who have treated scarcity as a design flaw rather than a law of nature.

paragraphs.subtitle: Why it matters
paragraphs.description: Competitive systems maximise for a narrow slice of outcomes and tend to externalise their costs. Cooperative systems, designed well, distribute the gains of shared infrastructure — roads, seeds, protocols, vocabularies — back into the community that maintains them.

paragraphs.subtitle: How it shows up
paragraphs.description: Cooperative patterns appear wherever people hold resources in common without fencing them off: seed libraries, tool libraries, open source, mutual aid networks, and the quiet everyday work of neighbours who know each other's names.

content.body: A regenerative system leaves its surroundings richer than it found them. It is the opposite of extractive: it invests surplus back into soil, water, trust, skills, and tools. Measured over generations, a regenerative system compounds abundance the same way an extractive system compounds debt.

A commons is any resource that a community takes collective responsibility for stewarding. Air, water, rivers, code, language, and public knowledge are all commons. The health of a commons is a direct measurement of the health of the community that holds it.
